ccel.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

title: Concerning Pleasures.

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: There are pleasures of the soul and pleasures of the body.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: The pleasures of the soul are those which are the exclusive possession of the soul, such as the pleasures of learning and contemplation.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: The pleasures of the body, however, are those which are enjoyed by soul and body in fellowship, and hence are called bodily pleasures: and such are the pleasures of food and intercourse and the like.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But one could not find any class of pleasures belonging solely to the body.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Again, some pleasures are true, others false.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And the exclusively intellectual pleasures consist in knowledge and contemplation, while the pleasures of the body depend upon sensation.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Further, of bodily pleasures, some are both natural and necessary, in the absence of which life is impossible, for example the pleasures of food which replenishes waste, and the pleasures of necessary clothing.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Others are natural but not necessary, as the pleasures of natural and lawful intercourse.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For though the function that these perform is to secure the permanence of the race as a whole, it is still possible to live a virgin life apart from them.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Others, however, are neither natural nor necessary, such as drunkenness, lust, and surfeiting to excess.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For these contribute neither to the maintenance of our own lives nor to the succession of the race, but on the contrary, are rather even a hindrance.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: He therefore that would live a life acceptable to God must follow after those pleasures which are both natural and necessary: and must give a secondary place to those which are natural but not necessary, and enjoy them only in fitting season, and manner, and measure; while the others must be altogether renounced.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Those then are to be considered moral pleasures which are not bound up with pain, and bring no cause for repentance, and result in no other harm and keep within the bounds of moderation, and do not draw us far away from serious occupations, nor make slaves of us.
